sections.content:
Now I see:
- Player: N at (24,40)-(27,42) - moved RIGHT and UP
- White strip: W at (24,43)-(27,43) - moved with player, now vertical
- New object: G at (24,44)-(27,47) with child b at (25,45)-(26,46) - this is a gray frame with one blue center!
- Merged object (lower part): c at (28,44)-(31,47) with child b at (29,45)-(30,46) - this is the lower part of the original merged object
- Carried square: c at (32,40)-(35,43) with child b at (33,41)-(34,42) - still separate
So the white strip merged with the top part of the merged object, creating a gray frame with one blue center. The original merged object split into two parts:
1. Top part: G at (24,44)-(27,47) with one blue center
2. Bottom part: c at (28,44)-(31,47) with one blue center
This is interesting. The white strip merged with the top part of the merged object, but the carried square is still separate at (32,40)-(35,43).
Now I need to merge the carried square with the bottom part of the merged object. The carried square is at (32,40)-(35,43), and the bottom part is at (28,44)-(31,47).
Let me try moving DOWN to merge the carried square with the bottom part.
Actually, the player is at (24,40)-(27,42), and the white strip is at (24,43)-(27,43). The carried square is at (32,40)-(35,43). If I move DOWN, the player and white strip will move DOWN, and the carried square might merge with the bottom part.
Let me try moving DOWN.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (24,40)-(27,42), carried square at (32,40)-(35,43)
# Bottom merged part at (28,44)-(31,47)
# Move DOWN to merge carried square with bottom part
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
